Facts.text: PopeJulius IIIproclaims new seat onCouncil of Trent
First play staged in Arcadia at Port-Royal, a performance of "Muses de la Nouvelle-France" by Marc Lescarbot
Pope Clement X beatifies Gorcumse martyrs
Gottfried Kirch discovers the Great Comet of 1680 (Kirch's Comet/Newton's Comet)
Spanish king Carlos appoints grandson prince Jozef Ferdinand as heir
First professional librarian in north America, Louis Timothee, hired in Philadelphia
Henry Fox appointed British 'Secretary of State for the Southern Department'
-15] Floods ravage Dutch coast provinces
Captain George Vancouver is first Englishman to enter San Francisco Bay
First streetcar (horse-drawn) (John Mason) debuts in NYC; fare 12 cents rode on 4th Avenue between Prince and 14th Streets
Skirmish at Danville, Mississippi
One of the last public executions in England: Franz Muller is hanged for murder in front of a crowd of 50,000 outside Newgate Prison
Leon Gambetta forms French government
Serbia invades Bulgaria, beginning the two-week Serbo-Bulgarian War; the Serbs are later defeated decisively [O.S. November 2]
St Andrews Golf Club, Yonkers NY, opens with just 6 holes
USC Trojans (then Methodists) play their 1st football game
Power plant at Niagara Falls begins operation
The Emancipation Run: 33 motorists parade from London to Brighton to celebrate England's Locomotives on Highways Act 1896, which increased automobile speed limit to 14 mph
David Belasco's "Girl of Golden West" premieres in NYC
Liberal candidate Jose Miguel Gomez wins national elections for president in Cuba
Oscar Strauss' musical "Der tapfere Soldat" premieres in Vienna
1st airplane flight from deck of a ship, Norfolk, Virginia
Billy Mallett of Hamilton Tigers kicks 10 singles in a game
Red Army captures Omsk, Siberia
American Pro Football League's Chic Tiger Joe Guyon punts 95 yards
Russia's Bolshevik army occupies Sevastopol, ending anti-communist attempts to regain control of the Russian government
The Communist Party of Spain is founded
German Weimar Republic Chancellor Joseph Wirth's term ends
Kentaro Suzuki completes his ascent of Mount Iizuna, Japan
World's largest gas tank in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, explodes; 28 die
Ottawa Mint Act is proclaimed in Britain
Nazis deprive German Jews of their citizenship
Dutch DC3 crashes at Schiphol, 6 die
Oil refinery fire kills 500 & destroys Lagunillas, Venezuela
During WW II, German planes destroy most of Coventry, England
British aircraft carrier Ark Royal sank in Mediterranean, having been torpedoed by a German submarine the day before
Governr-General Wouters of Dutch Antilles refuses Jews refuge
-Nov 15th) Japanese vs US sea battle at Savo-Island in Guadalcanal
Last Vichy-French troops in Algeria surrender
Chic Bear Sid Luckman passes for 7 touchdowns vs NY Giants (56-7)
J Postma, C Schalker, D Goulooze arrested for leading illegal CPN
H Lindsay and R Crouse's "State of the Union" premieres in NYC
Java: Sutan Sjahrir appointed to form a government as Indonesia's first Prime Minister
Dutch Dakota flight to Schiphol crashes, kills 11
First regular UK singles chart published by the New Musical Express
Greek General Alexander Papagos wins elections
WCIA TV channel 3 in Champaign, Illinois (CBS) begins broadcasting
Egyptian President Naguib resigns, state of emergency declared
Hungarian revolt put down by Soviet invasion
The Apalachin Meeting outside Binghamton, New York is raided by law enforcement, and many high level Mafia figures are arrested
Kilauea's most spectacular eruption (in Hawaii)
2 passenger trains collide at high-speed killing 118, and injuring 110, in Stéblová, Czechoslovakia
Belgium threatens to leave UN due to criticism on its policy on Congo
George Abbott Theater (Adelphi, 54 St) at 152 W 54th NYC, demolished
KCST TV channel 39 in San Diego, CA (NBC) begins broadcasting
US government sends 90,000 soldiers to Vietnam
Orchestre de Paris, France's 1st full-time salaried orchestra, debuts, conducted by its founder Charles Münch
The Congress of Colombia in commemoration of the 150 years of the death of Policarpa Salavarrieta, declares this day as "Day of the Colombian Woman"
"National Turn in Your Draft Card Day" features draft card burning
First European lung transplant
U.S. premiere of film version of Morris L. West's best seller "The Shoes of the Fisherman"
Yale University announces it is going co-educational
2nd Vietnam Moratorium Day in US
Apollo 12 (Conrad/Gordon/Bean) launched for 2nd manned Moon landing
Marshall U football team wiped out in DC-9 air crash at Kenova, West Virginia, killing 75
Enthronment of Pope Shenouda III as Pope of Alexandria
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 1,000 for 1st time (1003.16)
Canada begins production of Olympic coins
Spain, Morocco and Mauretania sign accord about Spanish Sahara
Cleveland Browns' Jerry Sherk sets club record with 4 sacks
California Angels Don Baylor, wins AL MVP
Guinea-Bissau Vice President João Bernardo "Nino" Vieira, overthrows President Luis Cabral in a military coup
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"Raise!" 11th studio album by Earth, Wind & Fire is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1982)
2nd NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Columbia 2 returns to Earth
Old Dutch Windmill in Golden Gate Park repaired and working again
Pakistan all out 62 v Australia at WACA, Lillee 5-18
Paul 'Bear' Bryant ties Amos Alonzo Stagg with 314 football wins
First cruise missile placed at Greenham Common, England
Astronauts aboard "Discovery" pluck a 2nd satellite from orbit
NASA launches NATO-3D
Zamboanga City mayor Cesar Climaco, a prominent critic of the government of Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos, is assassinated in his home city.
Brewers release 39-year-old pitcher Rollie Fingers
Volcano Nevado del Ruiz Colombia erupts, 1000s killed
Doubleday Publishing sells NY Mets to Nelson Doubleday and Fred Wilpon
FCC issues Notice Of Apparent Liability to WYSP Philadelphia
US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) imposes a record $100 million penalty against Ivan Boesky for insider trading
Sam's Town National Bowling Pro-Am won by Debbie Bennett
Sitcom "Murphy Brown" starring Candice Bergen premieres on CBS
Padres reliever Mark Davis wins NL Cy Young Award
Doug Drabek (22-6) wins NL Cy Young Award
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Philippines hit by typhoon, 110 die
Pittsburgh Steelers guard Terry Long loses his steroids appeal and is suspended without pay by NFL for 4 weeks
Puerto Rico votes against becoming the 51st US state
Space shuttle STS-66 (Atlantis 13), lands
Texas Ranger Juan Gonzalez wins AL MVP
Colo Rockie Larry Walker wins NL MVP
Disney's "Lion King" sets Broadway record of $2,700,000 daily sale
OPEC announces that it intends to cut its crude oil output quotas by 1.5 million barrels per day effective, but only if non-OPEC producers cut their output by 500,000 barrels per day as well
War in Afghanistan: Afghan Northern Alliance fighters takeover the capital Kabul
Argentina defaults on an $805 million World Bank payment.
the last direct-current distribution by Con Edison was shut down.
Eurozone officially slips into recession for the first time since its creation in 1999, pushed down by recessions in Germany and Italy
Hong Kong becomes the second Asian economy to tip into recession, its exports hit by weakening global demand
Italy plunges into recession, its first since the start of 2005, after GDP contracts a steeper-than-expected 0.5% in the third quarter
The National Statistical Service of Greece states that the country has been in recession since the beginning of the year
A series of protests against austerity measures occur across Europe including Spain, Portugal, and Greece
Discovery of CFBDSIR 2149-0403, the closest rogue planet to Earth, located a mere 100 light-years away
American harness racing trainer/driver Dave Palone sets the world record for wins when he drive Missy Tap Tina to the winner's circle for his 16,754th career victory
Second Democratic presidential candidates debate, broadcast by CBS, held in Des Moines, Iowa
7.8 earthquake cuts off town of Kaikoura, New Zealand, raising sea bed by 4m, and killing 2 people
Archaeologists announce discovery of ancient Greek city of Tenea near Corinth, supposedly founded by captives from Trojan War
Astronomers announce discovery of Super-Earth planet (3.2x bigger than Earth) orbiting red dwarf Barnard's star, 6 light years away
Attempt to move 720,000 Rohingya back to Myanmar from Bangladesh refugee camps amid international criticism. They refuse to go.
Edward Hopper's painting "Chop Suey" sells $92 million, a record for the artist at auction in New York
Large impact crater, 31 km wide, from iron meteorite identified under Hiawatha Glacier in Greenland

New York Mets pitcher Jacob deGrom wins NL Cy Young Award; his 10 wins fewest ever by a Cy Young winner in a non-strike-shortened season; MLB-leading 1.70 ERA; 29 consecutive start streak longest in MLB history
Record global daily total of 660,905 COVID-19 cases declared to the WHO, with total known infections over 54 million
Attack on a military police outpost near a gold mine in Inata, northern Burkina Faso, kills at least 53, prompting three days of national mourning
ICC Men's Cricket T20 World Cup, Dubai: Australia beats New Zealand by 8 wickets with 7 balls remaining for their first title; Player of the series: Australian batsman David Warner (289 runs)
Australian town of Eugowra experiences extreme flash flooding, with 2 deaths and 150 air-lifted to safety amid one-in-5,000-year flood event
Earliest evidence of fire being used to cook by humans found in study of fish remains 780,000 years ago at Gesher Benot Ya’aqov in northern Israel
Liberia General Election: Joseph Boakai defeats sitting President George Weah by just 1% in closest runoff in Liberia’s history
Mali's army seizes Kidal, a key town in the country's north, held by Tuareg rebels for nearly ten years
World's first protected area for endangered sperm whales set up off the coast of Caribbean island of Dominica
Chinese-backed deep-water megaport of Chancay in Peru is inaugurated by President Dina Boluarte and Chinese PremierXi Jinping
Maori MPs perform a haka, "Ka Mate", in New Zealand's parliament, suspending voting on a bill affecting the country's founding document, The Treaty of Waitangi / Te Tiriti o Waitangi
US Republicans re-gain control of the House of Representatives, they now control all three parts of US federal government, the House, Senate and the Presidency
Francisco Pizarrobegins his first great expedition, near Colombia
English writerSamuel Pepyswrites the first report of a blood transfusion (between dogs) in his diary
William Thomson, later scientist Lord Kelvin, enters Glasgow University at just 10 yrs 4 months
"Moby-Dick" byHerman Melvillefirst published by Harper and Brothers in the US
AmericanGail Bordenis issued a patent for technology related to his invention of condensed milk
Nathan Bedford Forrestis assigned to command of West Tennessee
Dion Boucicault's stage drama "The Shaughraun" opens at Wallack's Theatre, NYC
Charles J. Guiteauput on trial for the assassination of US President Garfield
"Treasure Island" byRobert Louis Stevensonis first published as a book by Cassell & Co.

New York World reporterNellie Bly(Elizabeth Cochrane) begins her attempt to surpass the fictitious journey ofJules Verne's Phileas Fogg by traveling around the world in under 80 days. She succeeds, finishing the trip in 72 days, 6 hours.
Gerhart Hauptmann's play "Hanneles Himmelfahrt" premieres in Berlin, Germany
US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltvisits Panama
The Third Duma (Parliament) meets in Russia; following TsarNicholas II's limiting of the franchise, a conservative majority holds sway and suppresses the radical elements
Albert Einsteinpresents his quantum theory of light
Tomáš Masarykdemands independence for Czechoslovakia
BBC begins daily radio broadcasts from the 2LO transmitter atMarconiHouse
FDRproclaims Philippine Islands a free commonwealth
Dick Hutton beatsLou Theszin Toronto, to become NWA wrestling champion
Milwaukee BraveHank Aaronwins NL MVP
Ray Charles' single "Georgia On My Mind" reaches #1
Betty Comden&Adolph GreenandJule Styne's musical "Fade Out-Fade In", goes into lay-off due to starCarol Burnett's illness
Detroit Red WingsGordie Howesets NHL record 627th career goal
Muhammad AliTKOs Cleveland Williams in 3 for heavyweight title
Jim Palmeris named AL Cy Young winner
"Network", directed bySidney Lumet, starringFaye Dunaway,Peter FinchandWilliam Holden, premieres in Los Angeles and New York City (Finch - Academy Awards Best Actor 1977)
Dutch war criminalPieter Mentencaptured one day after fleeing
Egyptian PresidentAnwar Sadatrepeats willingness to visit Israel toWalter Cronkite

In the first world championship fight between 2 Puerto Ricans in boxing history, WBC Super Welterweight champion Wilfred Benitez beats Carlos Santos in a 15-round unanimous decision at Caesar's Palace in Las Vegas
Polish Solidarity chairmanLech Wałęsafreed
James Worthyis arrested in Houston and charged with two counts of solicitation of prostitution
Michael Heseltine contestsMargaret Thatcher's leadership of the British Conservative Party
Cambodian PrinceNorodom Sihanoukreturns to Phnom Penh after thirteen years of exile
Michael Jackson's "Black or White" music video with groundbreaking morphing effects premieres simultaneously in 27 countries on MTV, Fox TV, and BBC's Top of the Pops
Don Shulabecomes the coach with the most wins in NFL history
Revival ofJohn KanderandFred Ebb's musical "Chicago" opens at Richard Rodgers Theater, NYC
Canadian singer-songwriterGeddy Leereleases his first solo album, "My Favourite Headache"
Film "Harry Potterand the Chamber of Secrets" is released, based on the 2nd book of the series byJ. K. Rowling
"Casino Royale", 21st James Bond film premieres in London, starringDaniel Craigfor the 1st time and Eva Green, premieres in London
Elizabeth Warrenis appointed to chair a Congressional Oversight Panel for the Emergency Economic Stabilization Act
Disney's 3-D computer-animated musical adventure film "Tangled" premieres at El Capitan Theatre, loosely based on the German fairy tale "Rapunzel" by brothers Jacob andWilhelm Grimm

German Red Bull driverSebastian Vettelwins the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix at Yas Marina Circuit to claim his first F1 World Drivers Championship by 4 points fromFernando Alonso; Red Bull's first Constructors title
"TheTwilight Saga: Breaking Dawn - Part 2", based on the book byStephenie Meyer, directed by Bill Condon, starringKristen StewartandRobert Pattinson, is released
Boston gangsterWhitey Bulgeris sentenced to two consecutive life terms plus five years for his crimes
"Moana" animated Disney film directed by Ron Clements and John Musker with voices by Auli'i Cravalho and The Rock premieres in Los Angeles
Toshiro Mifuneposthumously receives a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame at 6912 Hollywood Boulevard
US talk show "Tucker CarlsonTonight" hosted by Tucker Carlson premieres on Fox
Jewelry that belonged to French QueenMarie Antoinetteis auctioned off in Geneva, after not being seen for 200 years
UK Prime MinisterTheresa May’s cabinet approves draft plan for country's exit from the European Union (Brexit)
Thierry Henrybecomes manager of Major League Soccer side Montreal Impact
Grammy Award winnerRoberta Flackreveals she is suffering from ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease) and is no longer able to sing
US PresidentJoe Bidenand Chinese PresidentXi Jinpingmeet in person for the first time as national leaders at a G20 meeting in Bali, Indonesia

New Orleans elementary schools begin desegregation under an order from U.S. Circuit Judge J. Skelly Wright; Tessie Prevost, Gail Eitenne, and Leona Tate (known as "the McDonogh Three")  join McDonogh 19 Elementary School, and Ruby Bridges joins William Frantz Elementary School. They are met with death threats and racial slurs while the schools faced immediate boycotts.
American and British authorities announce indictments against two Libyan intelligence officials for the downing of Pan Am Flight 103
First public trains run through the Channel Tunnel, linking England and France under the English Channel
The United States House of Representatives votes not to create an independent commission to investigate the September 11 attacks
Zimbabwe Army seizes key sites in capital Harare following tensions overRobert Mugabe's dismissal of vice-president Emmerson Mnangagwa
Janus Secundus [Jan Everaerts], Dutch writer and Neo-Latin poet (Liber Basiorum (Book of Kisses)), born in The Hague, Habsburg Netherlands (d. 1536)
Maurice of Nassau, Dutch nobleman (Prince of Orange, 1618-25), and military leader in rebellion against Spain, born in Dillenburg, County of Nassau, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1625)
Jean Eudes, French catholic priest, founder of the Eudists, born at Ri, France (d. 1680)
Friedrich Wilhelm Zachow, German musician and composer (Georg Frideric Handel's master), baptized in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1712)
Omobono Stradivari, Italian violin maker and son of Antonius, born in Cremona, Duchy of Milan, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1742)
Leopold Mozart, German composer and teacher of his son Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, born in Augsburg (d. 1787)
Johann van Beethoven, German musician, Ludwig van Beethoven's father and first teacher, born in Mechelen, Habsburg Netherlands (d. 1792)
Giulio Gabrielli the Younger, Italian Cardinal (served Pius VII), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1822)
Gaspare Spontini, Italian opera composer and conductor (La Vestale), born in Maiolati, Papal State (d. 1851)
Henri Dutrochet, French physiologist (discovered & named process of osmosis), born in Néon, France (d. 1847)
Johann Nepomuk Hummel, Austrian piano virtuoso and composer, born in Pressburg, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1837)
Adam Oehlenschläger, Danish poet who wrote nation anthem "Der er et yndigt land", born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1850)
Carlo Conti, Italian composer, born in Arpino, Lazio, Papal States (d. 1868)
Heinrich Dorn, German composer and opera conductor, born in Königsberg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1892)
August Pott, German philologist (Etymology Forschungen), born in Nettelrede, Germany (d. 1887)
Jacob Abbott, American teacher and writer of children's books (the Rollo series), born in Hallowell, Maine (d. 1879)
Fanny Mendelssohn Hensel, German composer, and pianist, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1847)
Maria Christina of Savoy, Queen of the Two Sicilies and saint, born in Cagliari, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1836)
Michael Kelly Lawler, Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in County Kildare, Ireland (d. 1882)
John Curwen, English Congregationalist minister and founder of Tonic sol-fa music system, born in Heckmondwike, Yorkshire, England (d. 1880)
William Harrow, American lawyer and a controversial Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Winchester, Kentucky (d. 1872)
Robert Fruin, Dutch historian (Eighty Years' War), and professor (Leiden University), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1899)
August Reissmann, Prussian composer, born in Frankenstein, Silesia (now Ząbkowice Śląskie, Poland) (d. 1903)
Isaac Wistar, American lawyer, author, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1905)
James Birdseye MacPherson, American Major General (Union Army), born in Clyde, Ohio (d. 1864)
Giulio Roberti, Italian composer, born in Barge, Piedmont, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1891)
Antonius von der Linde, Dutch-German librarian and historian (history of chess), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1897)
August Senoa, Croatian writer (Zlatarevo zlato), born in Zagreb, Croatia (d. 1881)
Walter Williams, American, claimed to be last survivor of the US Civil War, born in Itawamba County, Mississippi, (d. 1959) date disputed, possibly 1854
Allen Hill, English cricket fast bowler (took 1st wicket in Test cricket; 2 Tests, 7 wickets; Yorkshire CCC), born in Kirkheaton, England (d. 1910)
Ernst Perabo, German-American composer and pianist, born in Wiesbaden, Duchy of Nassau (d. 1920)
Ulisse Dini, Italian mathematician and politician (Fondamenti per la teorica delle funzioni di variabili reali), born in Pisa, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1918)
Alexander Samsonov, Russian general (Russo-Japanese War; WWI - Battle of Tennenberg), born in Kherson Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1914)
Frederick Jackson Turner, American historian and educator (known for his "frontier thesis"), born in Portage, Wisconsin (d. 1932)
Steele Rudd [Arthur Hoey Davis], Australian author (On Our Selection), born in Drayton, Queensland, Australia (d. 1935)
Gregorio del Pilar, Filipino general known as the 'boy general' during the Philippine-American War, born in Bulakan, Philippines (d. 1899)
Jakob Schaffner, Swiss writer (Conrad Pilater), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1944)
René de Clerq, Flemish poet and author (Emergency Horn), born in Deerlijk, Belgium (d. 1932)
Julie Manet, French painter and model for the Impressionists, daughter of Berthe Morisot, born in Paris, France (d. 1966)
Leopold Staff, Polish Modernist poet, born in Lemberg, Austrian partition (d. 1957)
Geoffrey Turton Shaw, British church composer, born in London, England (d. 1943)
Eugene O'Brien, American actor (Rebecca of Sunnybrook Farm), born in Boulder, Colorado (d. 1966)
Nikolaos Plastiras, Greek politician and general (Prime Minister of Greece 1945, 50, 51-52), born in Karditsa, Greece (d. 1953)
Sonia Delaunay, Russian-born French artist (Orphism), born in Hradyzk, Russian Empire (d. 1979)
Bernhard Paumgartner, Austrian musicologist, conductor, and composer, born in Vienna, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1971)
Ted Meredith, American athlete (Olympic gold 800m in world record 1:51.9s 1912), born in Chester Heights, Pennsylvania (d. 1957)

Carlo Emilio Gadda, Italian writer (La Madonna dei filosofi - The Philosoper's Madonna; Giornale di guerra e di prigionia - War and Prison Diary), poet, and engineer (Vatican City power station), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1973)
Harold Collett Dent, British journalist, educationist, and editor of the Times Education Supplement, birthplace unknown (d. 1995)[1]
Seena Owen, American silent screen actress (Queen Kelly), born in Spokane, Washington (d. 1966)
Louise Huff, American actress (What Women Want), born in Columbus, Georgia (d. 1973)
Walter Jackson Freeman II, American physician who developed and practiced psychiatric surgery and transorbital lobotomy, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania(d. 1972)
Mamie Eisenhower, First Lady of the United States (1953-61), born in Boone, Iowa (d. 1979)
John Steuart Curry, American painter and lithographer (Baptism in Kansas), born in Dunavant, Kansas (d. 1946)
Benjamin Fondane, Romanian-French writer, filmmaker and critic, born in Iași, Kingdom of Romania (d. 1944)
Clara Fasano, Italian-American sculptor, born in Castellaneta, Italy (d. 1990)
(John) Morton Downey, American pop tenor, known as "The Irish Nightengale", songwriter, radio and television host, born in Wallingford, Connecticut (d. 1985)
Aleksey Semyonovich Zhivotov, Russian composer, born in Kazan, Russia (d. 1964)
Art Hodes, Ukrainian-American jazz and blues pianist, editor (Jazz Record), and educator, born in Nikolaev, Russian Empire (d. 1993)
Arthur Michael Ramsey, British Anglican bishop and 100th Archbishop of Canterbury, born in Cambridgeshire, England
Dick Powell, American singer, radio and screen actor (Cry Danger; Dick Powell Theater), producer and director (The Enemy Below), born in Mountain View, Arkansas (d. 1963)
Harold Larwood, England cricket fast bowler (21 Tests, Bodyline Series v Australia 1932-33), born in Nuncargate, England (d. 1995)
Marya Mannes, American author (The Reporter), born in New York City (d. 1990)
John Henry Barbee, American blues guitarist and singer, born in Henning, Tennessee (d. 1964)
Sanoesi Pane, Indonesian author and journalist (founded news agency Antara), born in Muara Sipongi, Tapanuli, Dutch East Indies (d. 1968)
Louise Brooks, American silent screen star (American Venus, Pandora's Box), born in Cherryvale, Kansas (d. 1985)
Howard William Hunter, the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, born in Boise, Idaho (d. 1995)
Pedro Arrupe, Spanish Jesuit priest (1st responder after the bombing of Hiroshima), born in Bilbao, Spain (d. 1991)
William Steig, American cartoonist (The New Yorker, 1930-2003) and children's author (Shrek!; Sylvester and the Magic Pebble; Doctor De Soto), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2003)[1]
Harrison Salisbury, American journalist and author (New York Times correspondent in Moscow), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1993)
Eric Malpass, English writer (Pentecost family books), born in Derby, England (d. 1996)
Norman MacCaig, Scottish poet, born in Edinburgh (d. 1996)

Rosemary DeCamp, American radio, film, and television character actress (Love That Bob - "Margaret", That Girl - "Mrs. Marie"; The Partridge Family - "Amanda Renfrew"), born in Prescott, Arizona (d. 2001)
Barbara Hutton 'poor little rich girl', American heiress (Woolworth fortune), born in New York City (d. 1979)
Eric Crozier, British theater producer and librettist (worked with Benjamin Britten), born in London, England (d. 1994)
Florentine Rost van Tonningen (née Heubel), Dutch Nazi supporter known as "Black Widow", born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2007)
Ken Carson, American western singer (Garry Moore Show), born in Coalgate, Oklahoma (d. 1994)
Edward Digby Baltzell, American Ivy League sociologist (coined term WASP), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Mabel Fairbanks, Seminole-African American figure skater and coach (US Figure Skating Hall of Fame), born in Florida Everglades (d. 2001)[1]
Martha Tilton, American big band singer (Benny Goodman; Jimmy Dorsey), born in Corpus Christi, Texas (d. 2006)[1]
George Fierstone, British jazz and session drummer (The Skyrockets), born in London, England (d. 1984)
Sherwood Schwartz, American television screenwriter and producer (The Brady Bunch, Gilligan's Island), born in Passaic, New Jersey (d. 2011)
Lisa Otto, German soprano (Berlin State Opera, 1951-85), born in Dresden, Germany (d. 2013)
Johnny Desmond [Giovanni De Simone], American radio and big band singer (Bob Crosby; Gene Krupa; Your Hit Parade; Glenn Miller's Army Air Forces Orchestra), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1985)[1]
Brian Keith, American actor (Family Affair, Loneliest Runner, The Parent Trap), born in Bayonne, New Jersey (d. 1997)
Marina Candael, Flemish dancer and choreography (Rhapsody in Blue), born in Antwerp, Flanders, Belgium (d. 2003)
Veronica Lake [Constance Ockleman], American actress (Sullivan's Travels, I Married a Witch), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1973)
Margaret Courtenay, Welsh stage and screen actress, singer and entertainer (Royal Flash; Duet for One), born in Cardiff, Glamorgan, Wales (d. 1996)
A. F. Shore, British Egyptologist (British Museum), born in Aldbury, England (d. 1994)
Billy Jim Layton, American composer and academic, born in Corsicana, Texas (d. 2004)
John J. Rigas, American entrepreneur and founder of Adelphia Communications Corporation, born in Wellsville, New York (d. 2021)[1]
Kenneth J. Gray, American politician (Rep-D-IL, 1955-74, 85-89), born in West Frankfort, Illinois (d. 2014)
Leonid Kogan, Soviet Russian violinist (Lenin Prize, 1952), born in Dnepropetrovsk, Ukrainian SSR, USSR (d. 1982)
Phyllis Avery, American actress (Ruby Gentry, George Gobel Show, Mr Novak), born in New York City (d. 2011)
McLean Stevenson, American actor (M*A*S*H, 1972-75 - "Col. Henry Blake"; Hello Larry), born in Normal, Illinois (d. 1996)
Narciso Yepes, Spanish classical guitarist (Orquesta Nacionale, 1947), born in Lorca, Spain (d. 1997)
Sir Lawrence "Lawrie" Barratt, British businessman and housing developer (Barratt Developments), born in England (d. 2012)
Kathleen Hughes [Elizabeth von Gerkan], American actress (It Came From Outer Space; Bracken's World), born in Hollywood, California (d. 2025)
Leopoldine "Leonie" Rysanek, Austrian dramatic soprano (Vienna State Opera 1954-96; Metropolitan Opera, 1959-96), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1998)
Gilbert Rogin, American journalist and author (The New Yorker, Sports Illustrated), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2017)
Horst Janssen, German graphic artist and printmaker, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1995)
Jimmy Piersall, American baseball outfielder (MLB All Star 1954, 56; his bipolar disorder subject book & film, "Fear Strikes Out"), born in Waterbury, Connecticut (d. 2017)
Alan Moss, English cricket fast bowler (9 Tests, 21 wickets; Middlesex CCC), born in London, England (d. 2019)
Dame Elisabeth Frink, British sculptor and printmaker, born in Thurlow, Suffolk, England (d. 1993)
Ed White, American USAF officer and test pilot, aeronautical engineer, and NASA astronaut (Gemini 4; Apollo 1), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 1967)
Ham Kee-yong, South Korean runner (Boston Marathon 1950), born in Chuncheon, South Korea (d. 2022)
Jay Migliori, saxophonist (Supersax), born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 2001)
Michael Robbins, British actor and comedian (On the Buses, The Great Muppet Caper), born in London, England (d. 1992)
Monique Mercure [Marie Monique Émond], French Canadian actress (Naked Lunch; J.A. Martin Photographer), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2020)
Ornelio Martina, Antillian author, and politician , born in Curacao
Peter Katin, British concert pianist, born in London (d. 2015)
Pierre Bergé, French industrialist and co-founder of Yves Saint Laurent Couture House, born in Oléron, France (d. 2017)
Shirley Crabtree Jr. [Big Daddy], English pro wrestler (record-breaking 64-inch chest), born in Halifax, England (d. 1997)
Terence Marsh, British production designer (Oliver!, Dr. Zhivago, Spaceballs), born in London, England (d. 2018)
(Hendricus) "Henk" Mochel, Dutch TV host (Rondom Tien), born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 2016)
Ramon Zupko, American classical and electro-acoustic composer (Fluxus), and educator (Western Michigan University, 1971-96), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2019)
